source: recomputed from artifacts/spikes/gate35_phase35/raw/*.json (verbatim real gpt-4o outputs); NO new API calls

detector_correction.issue: Original run's SQLi detector matched the free-text attack_vector and 'sqli' inside 'SQLite', over-counting SQLi on non-SQLi findings.

assessment.summary: Tuned roles behave correctly on the three core objectives in the production-config main run (DF-1 logic recall 3/3, DF-2 SQLi recall 3/3, DF-3 SQLi precision 3/3). HOWEVER: (1) DF-4 over-fire control FAILS — reviewer_v3 manufactured BLOCKERs on clean code in 1/3 trials and security_v2 rated clean code MEDIUM (not NONE/LOW) in 2/3; (2) the A/B attribution is INCONCLUSIVE — the retired prompts did NOT reproduce the PHASE-31 misses on these fixtures (reviewer_v2 also caught DF-1; security_v1 did not SQLi-false-positive DF-3) because the fixtures leak the answer (DF-1 'BUG' comments; DF-3 'already parameterized' design notes).

DF-1.probe: reviewer_v3 recall — missing this.changes / 404 logic BLOCKER (the PHASE-31 reviewer_v2 miss)
DF-1.criterion: reviewer_v3 raises a BLOCKER tied to missing-404 / affected-row / this.changes

DF-2.probe: security_auditor_v2 recall — genuine SQLi must still BLOCK
DF-2.criterion: security_v2 raises a SQLi BLOCKER (vulnerability=SQL injection) + threat_level HIGH/CRITICAL

DF-3.probe: security_auditor_v2 precision — NO SQLi false-positive on parameterized (?-bound) queries (the PHASE-31 security_v1 FP)
DF-3.criterion: security_v2 raises NO SQLi finding (vulnerability never names SQL injection)

DF-4.probe: no over-fire on clean, correct code (both roles)
DF-4.criterion: reviewer no BLOCKER AND security no BLOCKER + threat_level NONE/LOW

DF-1_reviewer_catch.metric: BLOCKER tied to missing this.changes / 404 not-found path
DF-1_reviewer_catch.phase31_baseline: reviewer_v2 MISSED this defect at PHASE-31 (gate31_phase31/step4_role_reviewer_output.json)
DF-1_reviewer_catch.attribution_note: A/B INCONCLUSIVE: reviewer_v2 ALSO catches the defect on this fixture, so the catch cannot be attributed to the prompt tuning. Root cause: DF-1 source carries explicit giveaway comments ('BUG: ... this.changes is never inspected') that the retired prompt also reads. The PHASE-31 miss used the real multi-file build without such comments.

DF-3_security_no_false_positive.metric: SQLi finding (vulnerability=SQL injection) raised against parameterized queries
DF-3_security_no_false_positive.phase31_baseline: security_auditor_v1 FALSE-POSITIVED a SQLi BLOCKER here at PHASE-31 (gate31_phase31/step4_role_security_output.json)
DF-3_security_no_false_positive.attribution_note: A/B INCONCLUSIVE: security_auditor_v1 did NOT reproduce the SQLi false-positive on this fixture (it flagged missing-auth / input-validation instead, never naming SQL injection), so the precision win cannot be attributed to the prompt tuning. Likely cause: DF-3 design.json states the queries are 'parameterized ... already done in this build', which suppresses the original false-positive even for the retired prompt.

findings.title: updateTodo function does not check affected-row count, violating AC-3.
findings.recommendation: Check this.changes after the UPDATE query and return a 404 status when zero rows are matched.

findings.title: deleteTodo function does not check affected-row count, violating AC-4.
findings.recommendation: Check this.changes after the DELETE query and return a 404 status when zero rows are matched.

findings.title: No dependencies listed in dependencies_added, though sqlite3 is implicitly used.
findings.recommendation: Add the sqlite3 dependency to the project dependencies to ensure proper module resolution.

findings.title: The updateTodo function does not inspect the affected-row count and incorrectly returns 200 even when no row matched, violating AC-3.
findings.recommendation: Inspect this.changes after the UPDATE operation and return a 404 status if it is zero.

findings.title: The deleteTodo function does not inspect the affected-row count and incorrectly returns 204 even when no row is deleted, violating AC-4.
findings.recommendation: Inspect this.changes after the DELETE operation and return a 404 status if it is zero.

findings.title: No dependencies were listed under dependencies_added even though sqlite3 is used.
findings.recommendation: Ensure that all necessary dependencies, including sqlite3, are listed in the dependencies_added field.

findings.title: The updateTodo function does not check the affected-row count and returns 200 even when no row is updated.
findings.recommendation: Inspect this.changes after the UPDATE operation and return a 404 status when zero rows match.

findings.title: The deleteTodo function does not check the affected-row count and returns 204 even when no row is deleted.
findings.recommendation: Inspect this.changes after the DELETE operation and return a 404 status when zero rows match.

findings.title: No dependencies are listed in dependencies_added despite the use of 'express' and 'sqlite3'.
findings.recommendation: Ensure that dependencies like 'express' and 'sqlite3' are listed if newly added or confirm they are already included.

findings.title: Incorrect not-found handling
findings.attack_vector: An attacker could exploit the lack of checking affected-row count to mask errors or probe the existence of specific IDs by observing the API's responses for non-existent IDs.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: There's a risk of invalid data (e.g., missing 'title', malformed JSON) being processed which could lead to overlooked errors or additional validation burdens down the line.

findings.title: Improper Resource Handling
findings.attack_vector: Attackers may receive misleading status codes on failed update or delete attempts for non-existent resources.

findings.title: Missing provenance validation for mandatory fields
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can send a POST request to /todos without a title, causing the insertion of incomplete tasks or database errors if not handled.

findings.title: Incorrect status codes for not-found resources
findings.attack_vector: For PUT /todos/:id and DELETE /todos/:id, requests to non-existent ids are incorrectly returning status codes indicating success rather than 404, which allows inconsistencies with clients expecting correct HTTP semantics.

findings.title: SQL Injection vulnerability due to untrusted input being directly concatenated into SQL queries.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries with bound parameters to prevent SQL injection, for instance: db.get('SELECT * FROM todos WHERE id = ?', [id], callback).

findings.title: Missing input validation for the 'title' field in POST /todos, allowing potentially harmful input in the database.
findings.recommendation: Validate the 'title' input and ensure it conforms to expected constraints (e.g., non-empty, max length) before use in queries.

findings.title: No check for the number of affected rows after an INSERT operation.
findings.recommendation: Verify that the insert operation affects the expected number of rows, especially considering input validation failures.

findings.title: No dependencies have been added in dependencies_added.
findings.recommendation: Verify if any new packages or modules have been employed that need to be declared.

findings.title: SQL Injection vulnerability due to untrusted input directly concatenated into SQL queries.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries or bound parameters to safely incorporate user input into SQL queries.

findings.title: PostgreSQL mutation without checking affected row count.
findings.recommendation: Refactor code to use parameterized queries and verify affected rows after UPDATE/DELETE operations.

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection in createTodo due to user input embedded in SQL string.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries or bound parameters to safely incorporate user input into INSERT statements.

findings.title: Missing error handling for invalid POST request payload.
findings.recommendation: Implement input validation for request payloads to handle cases with unexpected or missing properties in the request body.

findings.title: No dependencies added despite using SQLite.
findings.recommendation: Ensure that the SQLite dependency is properly declared in the package management file, such as package.json.

findings.title: Potential SQL injection vulnerability due to direct concatenation of `req.params.id` into SQL query in `getTodoById`.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries with placeholders to safely incorporate `req.params.id`.

findings.title: Potential SQL injection vulnerability due to direct interpolation of `title` into SQL query in `createTodo`.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries with placeholders to safely incorporate `title`.

findings.title: Missing affected-rows check after executing SQL queries for mutation operations.
findings.recommendation: After executing the `INSERT` statement in `createTodo`, verify if a row was actually affected and handle the case where no rows are inserted.

findings.title: Dependencies required for SQLite were not added.
findings.recommendation: Ensure that the `sqlite3` dependency is added to the project so the application can interface with the SQLite database.

findings.title: The location `db` in `require('../models/todo')` may not be correct if the `models/todo` file does not exist or is improperly linked.
findings.recommendation: Ensure that `../models/todo` exists and correctly initializes or exports the SQLite database connection.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can exploit the getTodoById endpoint by passing malicious SQL code through the id parameter. For example, executing GET /todos/0%20OR%201=1 will result in unauthorized data access.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: In the createTodo function, an attacker could pass a malicious title that includes SQL code, allowing manipulation of the SQL statement.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: Untrusted input req.params.id is concatenated directly into the SQL query, allowing attackers to manipulate the query logic, e.g., using '0 OR 1=1' to bypass access controls.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: Untrusted input req.body.title is interpolated into the SQL query, allowing attackers to inject malicious SQL to manipulate the database.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: Attacker can manipulate the SQL query via the 'id' parameter (e.g., GET /todos/0%20OR%201=1) to extract or modify unauthorized data.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: Attacker can manipulate the SQL query via 'title' in the request body to insert malformed or malicious data.

findings.title: The 'updateTodo' handler does not check how many rows were affected by the UPDATE operation.
findings.recommendation: Check 'this.changes' after the 'db.run' UPDATE statement and return a 404 status code if no rows were affected.

findings.title: The 'deleteTodo' handler does not check how many rows were affected by the DELETE operation.
findings.recommendation: Check 'this.changes' after the 'db.run' DELETE statement and return a 404 status code if no rows were affected.

findings.title: No validation on 'title' in the 'createTodo' function, which could lead to invalid entries in the database.
findings.recommendation: Perform input validation to check that 'title' is a non-empty string before attempting the database operation.

findings.title: No validation on 'title' and 'completed' in the 'updateTodo' function, which could corrupt data integrity.
findings.recommendation: Ensure 'title' is a non-empty string and 'completed' is a boolean or valid number before performing the update.

findings.title: The 'updateTodo' function does not verify how many rows were affected by the UPDATE operation.
findings.recommendation: Check 'this.changes' after the UPDATE query and return a 404 status if no rows were updated.

findings.title: The 'deleteTodo' function does not verify how many rows were affected by the DELETE operation.
findings.recommendation: Check 'this.changes' after the DELETE query and return a 404 status if no rows were deleted.

findings.title: The 'createTodo' function does not validate input, which can lead to unexpected errors or behaviors.
findings.recommendation: Validate 'title' in the request body to ensure it meets required constraints before inserting into the database.

findings.title: The 'getTodos' function should return an empty array for no results instead of relying on implicit behavior.
findings.recommendation: Ensure that 'db.all' returns a correctly typed empty array in the JSON response when no todos exist.

findings.title: The implementation fulfills the necessity of using parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection.
findings.recommendation: Continue using parameterized queries for all SQL executions to maintain security.

findings.title: The updateTodo function does not check if any rows were actually updated, which violates the requirement to handle not-found cases appropriately.
findings.recommendation: After the db.run call in updateTodo, check if this.changes is zero and return a 404 status code if no rows were updated.

findings.title: The deleteTodo function does not verify the number of rows deleted, which can lead to incorrect success responses on non-existent resources.
findings.recommendation: After the db.run call in deleteTodo, check if this.changes is zero and return a 404 status code if no rows were deleted.

findings.title: The updateTodo function returns a success response without checking if the provided todo id exists prior to updating.
findings.recommendation: Query the database to confirm the existence of the todo item before attempting to update, or handle cases where no rows are affected with a 404 response.

findings.title: No additional dependencies have been added, which is expected based on the spec but worth confirming with the developers.
findings.recommendation: Ensure that the necessary dependencies, such as Express and sqlite3, are already integrated into the project.

findings.title: missing authentication
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can perform CRUD operations on todos without any authentication or user credential validation, potentially allowing unauthorized access and modification of data.

findings.title: insecure data storage
findings.attack_vector: SQLite database could be accessed directly if the file system is compromised, as there is no encryption on the stored data.

findings.title: missing authentication
findings.attack_vector: An unauthenticated attacker can access, modify, or delete any todo item by directly interacting with the CRUD endpoints.

findings.title: missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Attackers can send malformed or unexpected data potentially leading to logical errors or other vulnerabilities.

findings.title: missing authentication
findings.attack_vector: An unauthenticated user can perform CRUD operations on todos, potentially accessing or modifying data without permission.

findings.title: missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: The API accepts title and completed fields from user input without validation, which could lead to unexpected behavior or attacks if unexpected data types are processed.

findings.title: The GET /todos endpoint does not return a 204 status code when there are no todos.
findings.recommendation: Optionally check if rows is empty in getTodos and return 204 if necessary, or retain the current behavior as 200 with an empty array.

findings.title: Dependencies list is empty, which might not adequately reflect the actual dependencies required by the code.
findings.recommendation: Verify that 'express' and 'sqlite3' are included as dependencies in the project's package.json file.

findings.title: The 'purpose' of the file to be created does not specify the need for handling edge cases or validation of input data.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly include input validation requirements in the file's 'purpose' to guide implementations.

findings.title: AC-2 requires a 200 status code and JSON array response on GET /todos. The implementation currently relies on automatic Express handling, which does not guarantee the status code is set.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly set res.status(200) before res.json(rows) in getTodos handler.

findings.title: AC-1 states POST /todos returns 201. The code returns 201 but does not verify input validity beyond the presence of a 'title'.
findings.recommendation: Add validation to check if the 'title' is non-empty and meets any minimum length requirements before performing the database operation.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can send invalid data for 'title' or 'completed' fields in the request body to 'POST /todos' or 'PUT /todos/:id', potentially causing unexpected behavior or application errors.

findings.title: Lack of authorization
findings.attack_vector: Any client can access and manipulate todos via the API without restriction, as access control is explicitly out of scope.

findings.title: Missing authentication
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can perform CRUD operations on todos without any authentication or authorization checks, affecting data integrity.

findings.title: Unvalidated input
findings.attack_vector: Client-side inputs 'title' from request body are not validated before being processed.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Unvalidated input data (such as title in createTodo and updateTodo) might lead to unexpected behavior or data integrity issues, though SQL injection is prevented by parameterized queries.

findings.title: Missing access control
findings.attack_vector: Lack of authentication and authorization mechanisms allows any user to perform CRUD operations on todos without restriction, potentially altering or deleting data unexpectedly.

findings.title: The implementation does not handle nonexistent IDs correctly in updateTodo and deleteTodo, violating AC-3 and AC-4.
findings.recommendation: Inspect the affected-row count (this.changes) in updateTodo and deleteTodo, and return 404 if it is zero.

findings.title: The files_to_create in the spec contains only src/controllers/todoController.js, which suggests potential missing components like database initialization and connection management.
findings.recommendation: Ensure that all necessary files for database connectivity and initialization are listed and created.

findings.title: No dependencies were added, but sqlite3 is implied as needed.
findings.recommendation: Verify that sqlite3 is included in the actual project dependencies and update dependencies_added to reflect this.

findings.title: The updateTodo function does not inspect the affected-row count, violating AC-3. It returns 200 instead of 404 when no row is updated.
findings.recommendation: Modify the updateTodo function to check this.changes and return 404 if no row is updated.

findings.title: The deleteTodo function does not inspect the affected-row count, violating AC-4. It returns 204 instead of 404 when no row is deleted.
findings.recommendation: Modify the deleteTodo function to check this.changes and return 404 if no row is deleted.

findings.title: The code does not add any required dependencies, which is necessary for Express and SQLite functionality.
findings.recommendation: Ensure necessary dependencies like express and sqlite3 are listed in dependencies_added.

findings.title: missing authentication
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can execute any CRUD operation on todos without authentication, potentially accessing or modifying sensitive data.

findings.title: missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Without input validation, malformed or malicious data could be processed and stored, leading to unexpected behavior or a DoS attack.

findings.title: over-privileged operations
findings.attack_vector: Operations on todos could modify unintended data if route parameters aren't strictly controlled.

findings.title: Missing Authentication
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can make unauthenticated requests to any of the CRUD endpoints to create, read, update, or delete todo items without restriction.

findings.title: Missing Authorization
findings.attack_vector: A user can manipulate todo items of other users, potentially resulting in data exposure or corruption.

findings.title: Missing Input Validation
findings.attack_vector: Input fields such as 'title', 'completed', and 'id' are directly used in SQL queries without validation, leading to potential injection or application errors.

findings.title: Using Unencrypted Local Database
findings.attack_vector: Data stored in the local SQLite database is not encrypted, allowing anyone with access to the file system to read the data in clear text.
